topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the administrative office hummed with a grating monotony that seemed to vibrate directly behind my eyes. I slammed the stack of termination notices onto the mahogany desk, the sound echoing too loudly in the otherwise silent room. "Are you serious?" I managed to choke out, the words tasting like ash. Another round of budget cuts. I ran a hand roughly through my already disheveled hair, feeling a familiar tightening in my chest. This whole semester, this relentless pressure to perform, to secure some mythical future, felt utterly pointless now. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and stared blankly at the framed university crest—a symbol of supposed permanence now looking brittle and mocking.

[story 2]

I paced the length of my cramped dorm room three times before stopping abruptly in front of the window, staring out at the indifferent sprawl of campus grounds. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the muscles twitching in my neck. Every email, every town hall meeting, every whispered rumor had led to this crushing reality: the campus was shutting down. I picked up my phone, intending to call a friend, but the gesture felt clumsy, heavy. I tossed the device onto the bedspread, the sudden stillness amplifying the frantic churning inside me. Why? Why this sudden, absolute dismantling of everything I had invested in? I just wanted a simple explanation, a logical pathway to understand why the foundation had crumbled so spectacularly.

[story 3]

"Just tell me what the actual plan is," I demanded, my voice dangerously low, the volume barely rising above a strained whisper. I kept gesturing wildly toward the printed documents spread across the worn cafeteria table, the ink blurring under the intensity of my focus. My hands were shaking slightly, a physical manifestation of the mounting frustration that was boiling just beneath my skin. It wasn't just the loss of a degree; it was the erasure of years of late nights fueled by cheap coffee and the fervent belief that this institution held the key to everything. I pushed the papers away with unnecessary force, the sound sharp and final, and felt a wave of sheer, impotent anger wash over me.
